text:
Admission Date:  1984-04-06              Discharge Date:   1974-04-28


Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:James
Chief Complaint:
biliary tract obstruction

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Gastroduodenotomy
Roux-en-Y with Cholecystoenterotomy
Bile Duct Biopsy
Placement of Cyber-knife Seeds


History of Present Illness:
This 85-year-old gentleman has been worked up extensively by me
in the last month for a persistent biliary tract obstruction
which has plagued him
for the last 5-6 months. He presented with jaundice and had an
indwelling biliary stent placed to relieve this. Malignant
appearing stricture has been worked up extensively by Dr. Kimberly
Ryan who has indicated he felt this was a malignant process.
However, we were unable to obtain brushings or biopsies to
confirm this through multiple interventional approaches in the
bile duct. Mr. Lee had a 30 pound weight loss as well as
anorexia and intermittent abdominal pain. Prior to this, he had
been a pretty vibrant man but clearly
he was suffering over the last 03-01 year.

I reviewed his images which showed a distended gallbladder with
very large stone within it which was compressing directly on the
duodenum as well as the bile duct. There was a suggestion of a
Mirizzi's syndrome type of problem from the ERCP as well as the
CT scan. However, his CA19-9 level was up at 68 and his ERCP
images were more suggestive of malignancy. Mr. Lee also
suffered an episode of stent cholangitis within the last month
and because of this, we collectively decided to take him to the
operating room to get a definitive
diagnosis as well as to hopefully treat the biliary tract
obstruction.


Past Medical History:
depression, hypertension, coronary artery disease status post
PTCA and 3 stent placements, osteoarthritis, colon
resection, bilateral total knee replacement, benign prostatic
hypertrophy, Parkinson's disease, REM behavior disorder,
obstructive sleep apnea, periodic limb movements and excessive
daytime sleepiness

PSH: bilateral total knee replacement briefly complicated by
some mild confusion in the PACU; complication of a previous ERCP
with stent change resulted in mild heart failure during his last
hospitalization


Social History:
non smoker, no alcohol or drug use


Family History:
Non-Contributory

VSS

noted, MMM,

amt of serosang drainange.

Neurologic:

attention,

DIAGNOSIS:

I. Celiac lymph node (A):
1. Adenocarcinoma, well differentiated, in fibroadipose tissue.
2. No lymph node tissue.
II. Gallstone; gross examination:
Gallstone (3.5 cm), mixed-type.
III. Bile duct (B):
Adenocarcinoma, well differentiated.
Note: The tumor is morphologically consistent with a biliary
origin.
.
CHEST (PORTABLE AP)  2013-08-04 5:15 AM
IMPRESSION: AP chest compared to 11-29 and 9.
Mediastinal vascular engorgement has improved since 08-02.
Lungs are grossly clear. Heart size top normal. Lateral aspect
of the left lower chest is excluded from the study. The other
pleural surfaces are normal. Nasogastric tube ends in the upper
stomach, but would need to be advanced at least 4 cm to move all
the side ports beyond the gastroesophageal junction. Right
jugular line tip projects over the mid SVC. No pneumothorax or
appreciable pleural effusion.


Brief Hospital Course:
This 85-year-old gentleman has been worked up extensively for a
persistent biliary tract obstruction. He went to the OR for:
1. Cholecystoenterostomy with Roux-en-Y formation.
2. Gastroenterostomy.
3. Celiac lymph node biopsy.
4. Fiducial gold seed placement for cyber knife therapy.

He remained in the ICU over night for close monitoring. Cardic
enzymes were cycled and negative. His volume status was
monitored and he was transfered out to the floor the next day.

Pain: He had an epidural for pain control. He was then switched
to IV Morphine PRN. Once tolerating a diet, he was transitioned
to PO meds.

Post-op confusion: He initially had some post-op diorientation.
Family members helped orient him and he became calmer and
oriented the further out from surgery.

GI/Abd: He was NPO, with IVF and a NGT. The NGT remained in
place until POD 3. His diet was slowly advanced thereafter. He
was tolerating a regular diet at time of discharge and reported
+flatus and +BM.
His staples were removed and steri strips applied. There was
some minimal spotty drainage on the right side that was covered
with gauze. It did not look angry, red and did not need to be
opened.

CV: He did not have any cardiac issue post-op. He continued on
his Lopressor. His Plavix was restarted on POD 3.

PT: Ashley Heath was seen by PT and rehab was recommended.

Pathology: Celiac lymph node (A): 1. Adenocarcinoma, well
differentiated, in fibroadipose tissue. 2. No lymph node tissue.
II. Gallstone. III. Bile duct (B):
Adenocarcinoma, well differentiated. Note: The tumor is
morphologically consistent with a biliary origin.
This information was not yet discussed with the patient. The
family does know and a meeting will be held in clinic.

Geriatrics was following along closely and helped manage his
overall post-op care, including to minimize delirium and
infection risk.

Medications on Admission:
Doxazosin 1 mg', Plavix 75 mg daily (currently on hold for
surgery), Lopressor 6.25 mg ', Zetia 10 mg ', Spironolactone 25
mg ', Clonazepam 0.5 mg ', Furosemide 40 mg ', Wellbutrin 100 mg
'
Geriatrics Recs (02-09): d/c benadryl, use protonix instead of
famotidine and d/c ppi after good po intake, do not use Ativan -
it can cause rebound agitation, use morphine instead of demerol,
d/c reglan - can make Parkinson's worse


Discharge Medications:
1. Heparin (Porcine) 5,000 unit/mL Solution Sig: One (1)
Injection TID (3 times a day).
2. Acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO Q6H (every 6
hours) as needed for fever.
3. Doxazosin 1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at bedtime).

4. Pantoprazole 40 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One
(1) Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO Q24H (every 24 hours).
5. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: 1.5 Tablets PO BID (2
times a day): hold HR<60, BP<100 .
6. Clopidogrel 75 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
7. Docusate Sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2
times a day).
8. Oxycodone-Acetaminophen 5-325 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO
Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed.
9. Bupropion 100 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a
day).
10. Zolpidem 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at bedtime).



Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
14391 Javier Street Suite 137
Lake Amandaview, MN 24331 - 4478 Sanchez View Apt. 624
Hendersonberg, SC 31191-7977

Discharge Diagnosis:
Biliary Stricture
Mirizzi's syndrome


Discharge Condition:
Good
Tolerating Diet
Pain Controlled
Incision with minimal drainage from right side.


Discharge Instructions:
Please call your doctor or return to the ER for any of the
following:
* You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or
tightness.
* New or worsening cough or wheezing.
* If you are vomitting and cannot keep in fluids or your
medications.
* You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomitting,
diarrhea or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry
mouth, rapid heartbeat or feeling dizzy or faint when standing.
* You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a
bowel movement.
* Your skin, or the whites of your eyes become yellow.
* Your pain is not improving within 8-12 hours or not gone
within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is
getting worse or is changing location or moving to your chest or

back.
* You have shaking chills, or a fever greater than 101.5 (F)
degrees or 38(C) degrees.
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that
concern you.
* Please resume all regular home medications and take any new
meds
as ordered.
* Continue to amubulate several times per day.
* It is OK to shower and wash. No tub baths or swimming. No
heavy lifting >10 lbs for 4 weeks.


Followup Instructions:
Please follow-up with Dr. Watts in 11-18 weeks. Call (317-222-1417 to schedule an appointment.
